infographics.title: Entity Decision Flowchart: LLC vs C-Corp vs S-Corp (SKIPPED)
infographics.prompt:
Create a clean, professional infographic flowchart for international entrepreneurs choosing a U.S. business entity. Use a warm color palette with navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and burnt orange (#D4622B) accents on a light cream (#F8F6F1) background.

The flowchart should start with 'Choosing Your U.S. Entity' at the top and branch based on these decision questions:

1. 'Do you want outside investors (venture capital, angel funding)?' → YES leads to C-Corp path, NO continues
2. 'Will you have 5+ employees in the first year?' → YES leads to C-Corp path, NO continues
3. 'Are you a single founder or small partnership (2-3 people)?' → YES leads to LLC path
4. 'Do you want pass-through taxation (no double tax)?' → YES leads to LLC or S-Corp path

Three endpoint boxes:
- LLC box (gold accent): 'Best for: Solo founders, simple operations. Tax: Pass-through (no double tax). Filing: $125 in Florida. Think of it as: Your storefront.'
- C-Corp box (navy accent): 'Best for: Investor-backed startups. Tax: Corporate rate (21%) + dividend tax. Filing: $70-$300. Think of it as: Your tower.'
- S-Corp box (burnt orange accent): 'Best for: Established businesses with U.S. tax residents. Tax: Pass-through with payroll savings. Limit: Must have U.S. shareholders.'

Note at bottom: 'Most international founders start with an LLC. You can always convert later.'

Style: Modern, minimal, professional. Use rounded rectangles for boxes, clear arrows, readable sans-serif font. No decorative illustrations — purely informational. Include small icons (building, briefcase, chart) next to each entity type.
infographics.insertAfter: Comparison card block (LLC vs C-Corp vs S-Corp)

infographics.title: SunBiz Registration: 8-Step Process
infographics.prompt:
Create a vertical step-by-step process infographic showing Florida's SunBiz business registration process. Use navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and burnt orange (#D4622B) on cream (#F8F6F1) background.

Title: 'Registering Your Business on SunBiz.org — Step by Step'

8 steps in a vertical flow with numbered circles and brief descriptions:

1. CHOOSE YOUR ENTITY TYPE — LLC, Corp, or LP. Most international founders choose LLC.
2. SEARCH FOR NAME AVAILABILITY — Use SunBiz name search. Must be unique in Florida.
3. DESIGNATE A REGISTERED AGENT — Must have a Florida street address. You can use a service (~$100-$150/year).
4. FILE ARTICLES OF ORGANIZATION — Online via SunBiz. LLC filing fee: $125. Processing: 1-3 business days online.
5. OBTAIN YOUR EIN — Apply free at IRS.gov (SS-4 form). This is your business's 'social security number.'
6. OPEN A BUSINESS BANK ACCOUNT — Bring: Articles of Organization, EIN letter, passport, Operating Agreement.
7. REGISTER FOR STATE TAXES — If selling goods: register for Florida Sales Tax (DR-1 form). No state income tax registration needed.
8. OBTAIN LOCAL LICENSES — Check county/city requirements. Orange County: Local Business Tax Receipt ($25-$50).

Timeline indicator on the side: 'Total time: 1-2 weeks if prepared'
Cost summary at bottom: 'Estimated startup cost: $300-$500 (filing + registered agent + licenses)'

Style: Clean vertical timeline, each step in its own card/box, numbered prominently. Professional and clear, easy to follow top-to-bottom.

infographics.title: VAT vs. U.S. Sales Tax — Side-by-Side Comparison
infographics.prompt:
Create a professional side-by-side comparison infographic: 'VAT vs. U.S. Sales Tax — What's Different?' Use navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and burnt orange (#D4622B) on cream (#F8F6F1) background.

Two columns, each with a large header:

LEFT COLUMN — 'VAT (Value Added Tax)' in navy:
- Where: Most of the world (EU, LATAM, etc.)
- Applied: At EVERY stage of production
- Who collects: Every business in the supply chain
- Rate: National (one rate per country, typically 10-25%)
- Included in price: YES — consumer sees final price
- Filing: One national tax authority
- Example: Brazil (ICMS + IPI + PIS/COFINS = complex multi-layer system)

RIGHT COLUMN — 'U.S. Sales Tax' in amber:
- Where: United States (45 states + DC)
- Applied: ONLY at final sale to consumer
- Who collects: Only the final seller
- Rate: Varies by STATE + COUNTY + CITY (can be 0% to 10.25%)
- Included in price: NO — added at checkout
- Filing: Each state separately (nexus rules apply)
- Example: Florida = 6% state + up to 2.5% county surtax

Center divider with key insight: 'The biggest shock for international founders: prices on the shelf are NOT what you pay at the register.'

Bottom callout: 'Florida: 6% base rate. No tax on most services. Exemptions for manufacturing equipment and some food items.'

Style: Clean two-column layout, icons for each comparison point, professional and scannable.
infographics.insertAfter: Text section about VAT vs Sales Tax differences

infographics.title: U.S. Trademark Registration Timeline
infographics.prompt:
Create a horizontal timeline infographic showing the U.S. trademark registration process. Use navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and burnt orange (#D4622B) on cream (#F8F6F1) background.

Title: 'Trademark Registration Timeline — USPTO'

Timeline from left to right with time markers:

Month 0: FILE APPLICATION — TEAS Plus form ($250/class) or TEAS Standard ($350/class). Include: mark, goods/services description, specimen of use.

Month 1-3: USPTO REVIEW — Examining attorney reviews for conflicts. Average wait: 3-4 months.

Month 4-6: OFFICE ACTIONS (if any) — You may get a refusal or request for clarification. 3-month deadline to respond.

Month 7-8: PUBLICATION — Mark published in Official Gazette for 30 days. Anyone can oppose.

Month 9-12: REGISTRATION — If no opposition: registration certificate issued. Total time: 8-12 months typical.

After Registration: MAINTAIN — Declaration of Use at year 5-6 and renewal every 10 years.

Cost summary box: 'Total cost: $250-$750 for filing + $0 if self-filed, $1,500-$3,000 with attorney'

Key callout: 'International founders: Your home country trademark does NOT protect you in the U.S. IP rights are territorial — you must register separately.'

Style: Clean horizontal timeline with milestone markers, subtle progress bar connecting the steps.

infographics.title: Business Insurance Types — What You Need
infographics.prompt:
Create a professional infographic grid showing essential business insurance types for international founders in the U.S. Use navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and burnt orange (#D4622B) on cream (#F8F6F1) background.

Title: 'Business Insurance — What Coverage Do You Need?'

Grid of 6 insurance type cards (2x3 or 3x2 layout):

1. GENERAL LIABILITY (navy) — Required by most: Covers customer injuries, property damage, lawsuits. Cost: $400-$1,500/year. Get this FIRST.

2. PROFESSIONAL LIABILITY / E&O (amber) — For service businesses: Covers mistakes, negligence claims, missed deadlines. Cost: $500-$2,500/year. Required if you give advice or provide professional services.

3. WORKERS' COMPENSATION (burnt orange) — Required in Florida (4+ employees): Covers employee injuries on the job. Cost: varies by industry and payroll. Mandatory — not optional.

4. COMMERCIAL PROPERTY (medium blue) — If you have a physical space: Covers equipment, inventory, office contents. Cost: $500-$3,000/year. Includes fire, theft, some natural disasters.

5. BUSINESS OWNER'S POLICY / BOP (gold) — Best value for small businesses: Bundles general liability + property insurance at a discount. Cost: $500-$2,000/year. Great starting point.

6. CYBER LIABILITY (dark teal) — For any business handling customer data: Covers data breaches, ransomware, notification costs. Cost: $500-$5,000/year. Increasingly essential.

Bottom section: 'How to Buy' — Get quotes from 3+ providers, use an independent insurance broker, review annually.

Decision helper at very bottom: 'Just starting? Get General Liability first. Adding employees? Add Workers' Comp. Handling data? Add Cyber Liability.'

Style: Card-based grid, each card has an icon, the insurance name, key description, and price range. Color-coded borders per card.
infographics.insertAfter: Last content section before knowledge check

infographics.title: Visa Pathway Comparison Chart
infographics.prompt:
Create a professional comparison infographic showing the 4 main visa pathways for international entrepreneurs entering the U.S. Use navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and burnt orange (#D4622B) on cream (#F8F6F1) background.

Title: 'Four Visa Pathways for International Founders'
Subtitle: 'Four backstage passes, four different doors'

4 columns or large cards, one per visa:

1. E-2 TREATY INVESTOR (gold border):
- What: Investor visa for treaty country nationals
- Investment: $100,000+ typical (substantial)
- Duration: 2 years, renewable indefinitely
- Key requirement: Treaty country + substantial investment + active management
- Can hire employees: YES
- Path to green card: NO (must convert to another visa)
- Best for: Founders with capital from treaty countries

2. L-1A INTRACOMPANY TRANSFER (navy border):
- What: Transfer from your foreign company to U.S. office
- Investment: Must have existing foreign business
- Duration: 1-3 years initially, up to 7 years
- Key requirement: Managerial role + company existed 1+ year abroad
- Can hire employees: YES
- Path to green card: YES (EB-1C)
- Best for: Expanding an existing business to the U.S.

3. O-1A EXTRAORDINARY ABILITY (burnt orange border):
- What: For individuals with extraordinary achievement
- Investment: None required
- Duration: 3 years, renewable
- Key requirement: Prove extraordinary ability (awards, press, high salary, etc.)
- Can hire employees: Through a U.S. employer/agent
- Path to green card: YES (EB-1A)
- Best for: Founders with notable achievements, press coverage, or industry recognition

4. B-1/B-2 VISITOR (gray border):
- What: Business visitor (temporary)
- Investment: None
- Duration: Up to 6 months
- Key requirement: Cannot work or earn U.S. income
- Can hire employees: NO
- Path to green card: NO
- Best for: Initial market research, meetings, conferences ONLY

Bottom warning box: 'Working without proper visa authorization is a serious violation. Consult an immigration attorney before making business decisions.'

Style: Clean 4-column comparison, consistent rows for easy scanning, color-coded headers.

infographics.title: Cultural Missteps That Cost Real Money
infographics.prompt:
Create a professional infographic showing common cultural missteps that cost international founders real money in the U.S. market. Use navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and red (#C0392B) for warning accents on cream (#F8F6F1) background.

Title: 'Cultural Missteps That Cost Real Money'
Subtitle: 'Small misunderstandings, big consequences'

5-6 misstep cards arranged in a grid, each with a dollar cost indicator:

1. MISSING THE FOLLOW-UP WINDOW — '$5,000-$50,000 in lost deals'
U.S. partners expect a follow-up email within 24-48 hours. In many cultures, waiting shows respect. In the U.S., waiting = you're not interested.

2. HANDSHAKE DEALS WITHOUT CONTRACTS — '$10,000-$100,000+ in disputes'
In the U.S., if it's not in writing, it doesn't exist. Even friendly business relationships need signed agreements.

3. OVER-PROMISING TIMELINES — '$2,000-$20,000 in credibility damage'
Saying 'yes' to every deadline to be polite, then missing them. Americans plan around your committed dates.

4. IGNORING SMALL TALK — '$0 direct but relationship-killing'
Skipping the 2-3 minutes of weather/sports/weekend chat before business. Americans use this to build trust.

5. WRONG FORMALITY LEVEL — '$0 direct but deal-slowing'
Using overly formal titles (Sr. Director, Dr.) when Americans use first names, or being too casual too soon in formal industries (law, finance).

6. ASSUMING 'SOUNDS GREAT' MEANS YES — '$5,000-$25,000 in wasted preparation'
In U.S. business culture, 'Let me think about it' and 'Sounds interesting' usually mean NO. 'Let's do it' or a signed document means yes.

Bottom insight: 'The good news: Americans are forgiving of cultural differences. They care far more about reliability and follow-through than perfect etiquette.'

Style: Warning-style cards with yellow/red accents, dollar amounts prominently displayed, icons for each misstep. Professional but attention-grabbing.

maps.title: Central Florida Business Resources Map
maps.prompt:
Create a clean, illustrated map of the Central Florida / Greater Orlando region showing key business resources for international entrepreneurs. Use navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and burnt orange (#D4622B) on cream (#F8F6F1) background.

Title: 'Your Central Florida Resource Network'

Show the Greater Orlando area with these labeled locations:

1. UCF BUSINESS INCUBATION PROGRAM (gold star) — UCF main campus area, east Orlando. 'Free mentoring, workshops, office space for startups.'

2. SMALL BUSINESS DEVELOPMENT CENTER / SBDC (navy marker) — Downtown Orlando area. 'Free 1:1 consulting, business plan help, market research.'

3. SCORE ORLANDO (burnt orange marker) — Downtown Orlando. 'Free mentoring from retired business executives.'

4. ENTERPRISE FLORIDA (medium blue marker) — State organization. 'International trade assistance, export development.'

5. ORLANDO ECONOMIC PARTNERSHIP (teal marker) — Central Orlando. 'Regional economic development, relocation assistance, industry connections.'

Key highways labeled: I-4 corridor, 408 (East-West Expressway), 417 (Central Florida GreeneWay)

Landmarks for orientation: Orlando International Airport (MCO), UCF campus, Downtown Orlando, Lake Nona, International Drive area

Bottom legend: Color-coded markers with brief description of each organization type.

Note: 'All organizations listed offer FREE or low-cost services for international entrepreneurs.'

Style: Simplified illustrated map (not Google Maps style), warm colors, clear labels, professional cartographic illustration feel. Geographic accuracy matters but artistic interpretation is fine.

maps.title: Central Florida Networking Hotspots
maps.prompt:
Create a clean illustrated map of the Central Florida / Greater Orlando region showing key networking locations for international founders. Use navy blue (#1A3A5C), amber/gold (#E8A838), and burnt orange (#D4622B) on cream (#F8F6F1) background.

Title: 'Where to Show Up — Central Florida Networking Map'

Show the Greater Orlando area with these categories of locations:

EVENTS & CONFERENCES (gold markers):
- Orlando Tech Week (Downtown)
- 1 Million Cups Orlando (weekly, Downtown)
- Startup Weekend Orlando
- Orlando Regional Chamber events

COWORKING & INCUBATORS (navy markers):
- StarterStudio (Downtown Orlando)
- Canvs (Downtown)
- Groundswell Startups (Melbourne/Space Coast)
- UCF Business Incubation Program (East Orlando)
- Advent Health Innovation Lab (Lake Nona)

KEY AREAS (highlighted zones):
- Downtown Orlando (densest concentration)
- Lake Nona / Medical City (health tech)
- Research Park at UCF (tech/simulation)
- International Drive corridor (hospitality tech)
- Space Coast / Cape Canaveral (aerospace)

Bottom tip: 'Start with 1 Million Cups (free, weekly, casual) and one coworking space. Build from there.'

Style: Warm illustrated map style, zone highlights as semi-transparent overlays, clear category legend. Geographic accuracy with artistic interpretation.
maps.insertAfter: New accordion block (Where to Show Up)

prompts.qwenPrompt: Cinematic digital painting, a Caucasian woman in her early 40s and a Hispanic Latino man in his mid-30s seated across from each other at a round table in a modern coworking space meeting room in Central Florida, both in professional business casual attire. The man sits slightly back, listening attentively with a polite smile, holding a leather notebook. Between them on the table are coffee cups and a few printed documents. The meeting room has glass walls showing the coworking space beyond, with tropical plants and modern furniture visible. The scene captures the contrast between direct American communication style and the more formal approach common in Latin American business culture. Dramatic golden hour lighting through floor-to-ceiling windows, painterly brushstrokes visible throughout, atmospheric haze, warm saturated colors with amber and deep blue tones, concept art quality, rich and atmospheric. No text, no words, no letters. Wide composition, 16:9 aspect ratio. NOT a photograph, NOT photorealistic.
prompts.insertAfter: Opening text section about communication differences

prompts.title: Networking Event — Elevator Pitch Moment
prompts.qwenPrompt: Cinematic digital painting, a distinguished Hispanic Latino man in his early 50s at a professional networking event in a modern Central Florida venue, wearing a well-fitted charcoal gray suit with an open-collar pale blue shirt, his right hand holding a business card he is offering to another professional and his left hand holding a small plate with appetizers.
